🟥 Hating the Corrupt: Why Filipinos Settle for Rage Instead of Real Accountability

[MCT | Commentary]

The Philippines is simmering with a particular kind of anger these days. 

Scroll through social media and you’ll find something disturbing: people openly wishing death upon corrupt officials. 

Not imprisonment. Not dismissal from office. Not even the recovery of stolen wealth. 

Death—quick, violent, and extrajudicial.

This isn’t new. It’s the residue of the Duterte years, when extrajudicial killings became normalized as a solution to government failure. 

Between 2016 and 2022, Rodrigo Duterte’s “war on drugs” left between 6,000 and 7,000 dead by official count—possibly as high as 30,000 according to human rights estimates. 

What shocked international observers didn’t shock most Filipinos. 

Duterte maintained extraordinarily high approval ratings even as citizens were being gunned down on the streets by police and vigilantes. 

Many people quietly accepted the idea that when institutions fail, killing is an acceptable substitute for justice.

Then came the flood-control corruption scandal of 2025. 

Billions of pesos in public funds, lost in a maze of ghost projects, padded contracts, and kickbacks, with over 400 fake or dubious projects flagged in some reports. 

The public responded with massive protests, especially among the youth, demanding accountability and systemic change. 

But underneath the rallies, something darker was bubbling: some groups began explicitly calling for the death penalty for large-scale corruption, framing it as the only “fair” response to grand theft of public funds.

And then, in the middle of all that anger, came a moment that captured the mood almost too perfectly.

🟥 Kara David’s birthday wish

When veteran journalist Kara David turned 52 in September 2025, her birthday wish was caught on video. Laughing, in a light showbiz setting, she said: “Wish, sana mamatay lahat ng kurakot sa Pilipinas,” mimicking a familiar TV pose. 

The room laughed. The clip went viral. Headlines ran with her words. Comment sections filled up with people saying: “Sana nga.” “Amen.” “Best wish ever.”

It was joked about, memed, and half-framed as hyperbole. But the applause was real. 

The relatability was real. Many netizens openly agreed that her wish—that all the corrupt in the Philippines would die—was not just entertaining, but morally right.

Some editorials pushed back, warning that wishing death on anyone, even the corrupt, crosses a line and flirts with the same dehumanization that fueled the drug war. 

But that response was drowned out by the louder, more visceral reaction: “Kung hindi mamatay, hindi magbabago.” Kara’s wish worked because it said out loud what a lot of Filipinos now feel but rarely admit in public.

It’s one thing when anonymous accounts wish death on politicians. It’s another when a respected, award-winning journalist articulates that same wish, and the country nods along.

🟥 The longing for the Alex Boncayao Brigade

What makes this moment even more unsettling is what people our age are saying in private chats and comment sections.

Some of us grew up hearing stories about the Alex Boncayao Brigade (ABB)—the so‑called sparrow units of the 1980s and 1990s. 

They were the urban assassination arm of the communist insurgency, operating in Metro Manila and other cities, targeting policemen, soldiers, and figures linked to abuses and exploitation. 

At their height, they carried out dozens of assassinations a year, shooting targets at close range in crowded public spaces, then disappearing into the crowd.

Today, with corruption scandals piling up and dynasties as strong as ever, there’s a quiet nostalgia for that kind of “swift justice.” You see comments like:

- “Kung andiyan pa siguro ang ABB, hindi ganito kayabang mga kurakot.”
- “Baka kailangan na ulit ng sparrow units para matakot ‘tong mga pulPolitiko.”
- “Wala nang takot kasi wala nang ABB.”

It sounds like dark humor, but it’s not just a joke. It’s a confession. 

People my age (and older)—who should be the ones most invested in due process and human rights—are openly half-wishing for the return of a group literally known for urban assassinations. 

A death squad is being remembered like an old, effective shortcut we foolishly discarded.

So on one end you have Kara David’s now-iconic line: “Sana mamatay lahat ng kurakot sa Pilipinas.” 

On the other, you have ordinary citizens saying: “Sana may ABB ulit.” 

Put together, they show where our hearts really are right now—not in the courts, not in Congress, not in the slow machinery of law, but in fantasies of violent, final endings.

🟥 The justice system has failed us

The truth is, this obsession with death isn’t coming from nowhere. It’s coming from a justice system that has made legal accountability feel impossible.

Take the Sandiganbayan, the court specifically created to try graft and corruption cases. 

Studies show its acquittal rate in certain periods reaching above 75 percent—meaning most accused public officials walk free. 

One diagnostic report on corruption cases found a conviction rate of just 18.06 percent, while dismissals and archival account for roughly two-thirds of cases. 

On top of that, the average duration of a corruption case is around 6.6 years from filing to resolution.

That’s the average. 

Some cases, like those involving the Marcoses, have dragged on for nearly four decades without full resolution. 

Others quietly die when the accused dies of old age, or when technicalities and procedural delays push them beyond the ten-year prescriptive period under our anti-graft laws.

Even before cases reach the Sandiganbayan, overall conviction rates in the criminal justice system remain dismally low—often under 20 percent. 

Investigations are sloppy or under-resourced. 

Prosecutors are overburdened or compromised. 

Judges are overwhelmed, under-supported, or sometimes beholden to political networks. 

The message to the public is clear: if you are powerful and connected, the courts will not catch you. And if they do, they might still let you go.

On paper, we have laws.

Republic Act 3019, the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act, prescribes imprisonment, perpetual disqualification from public office, and even forfeiture of unexplained wealth. 

But on the ground, enforcement is weak. 

The Ombudsman lacks sufficient resources. The Commission on Audit (COA) flags anomalies but has no direct teeth. 

Oversight bodies are often dependent on the very politicians they are supposed to watch.

So what do people conclude? 

That the law exists, but only for the poor and the powerless.

That corruption cases are more likely to end in acquittal, dismissal, or endless delay than in actual punishment. 

That stolen money will stay in family accounts, hidden behind shell corporations, even if a token conviction happens.

Given all that, is it really surprising that people start fantasizing about ABB or laughing along with a wish that all corrupt should die?

🟥 Duterte and the normalization of shortcuts

The Duterte era took this simmering impatience and weaponized it. 

Duterte framed courts, due process, and human rights as obstacles to “real” justice. 

He told police to kill drug suspects if they resisted and publicly promised pardons to officers who would be charged. 

Night after night, bodies appeared on sidewalks with cardboard signs reading “pusher” or “addict,” often killed by unidentified assailants.

International groups and local advocates called it what it was: a campaign of extrajudicial killings. 

But many Filipinos saw something else: results. 

Drug pushers gone. 
Criminals “afraid.” 
A president who looked decisive, unconstrained by “slow” courts. 

Studies show that high levels of fear of crime, combined with distrust in the justice system, increased public support for Duterte’s violent policies.

This is the dangerous precedent. The drug war taught people that when the system is broken, killing is a valid option. 

That due process is a luxury. 
That human rights are for criminals, not for victims. 

It normalized the idea that violence is not only acceptable, but efficient.

Fast forward to 2025. Replace “drug suspects” with “corrupt officials.” 

Replace “Tokhang” with nostalgia for ABB. 
Replace Duterte’s speeches with Kara David’s one-liner and viral comment threads. 

It’s the same logic applied to a different target.

🟥 Political dynasties and the architecture of impunity

All of this is made worse by the way power is actually structured in the Philippines. 

Corruption here is not just personal greed—it’s the operating system of political dynasties. 

Families capture local and national positions, pass seats from parent to child to cousin, and construct networks of loyalty that shield them from accountability.

Academic work and policy analyses describe how pakikisama and family loyalty, usually beautiful Filipino values, morph into a code of silence when it comes to graft. 

Whistleblowers risk social and physical harm. Witnesses are intimidated or bought. 

Prosecutors and judges are pressured. 

Political clans agree, implicitly or explicitly, not to fatally wound one another because they all benefit from the same loose, permissive system.

The result? A culture in which corruption is seen as inevitable, almost natural. 

Surveys and studies note that large majorities of Filipinos believe corruption is widespread and that “honesty” in politics is more the exception than the rule. 

Some commentaries bluntly say that tolerance for corruption has become ingrained in the Filipino psyche, not because we like it, but because we’ve adapted to an environment where fighting it feels futile.

So when someone like Kara David, who has seen poverty and injustice up close for decades, says “sana mamatay lahat ng kurakot,” people don’t gasp. They nod. 

When people our age talk half-seriously about bringing back ABB, they’re not just glorifying violence—they’re expressing disbelief that the current system can ever produce real justice.

🟥 This is not an endorsement

This is not an endorsement of violence, extrajudicial killings, death squads, or the return of groups like the Alex Boncayao Brigade, which has been tagged internationally as a terrorist organization. 

The point of naming these wishes and fantasies is to show how deep the frustration has gone—and to argue that even in this anger, the only path worth fighting for is human rights, due process, and a justice system that actually works.

🟥 What we should be demanding instead

The hatred of corrupt officials is justified. The anger is legitimate. But right now, we’re spending that energy on death wishes instead of structural change.

The harder, more meaningful demands should be things like:

- Fixing the justice system.  
  Strengthen the Ombudsman and prosecution service so corruption cases are investigated well and filed quickly, with enough lawyers and investigators to match the scale of the problem. Reform Sandiganbayan procedures so cases don’t drag for most of a decade, and close the loopholes that allow powerful defendants to stall endlessly.

- Speed and certainty of punishment.  
  Aim to resolve corruption cases in three to five years, not ten or twenty. Enforce automatic forfeiture of ill-gotten wealth and truly implement lifetime bans from office for those convicted under RA 3019.

- Real transparency.  
  Publish all government contracts and expose beneficial owners of companies that win public bidding. Audit Statements of Assets, Liabilities, and Net Worth (SALNs) regularly and publicly, with consequences for those who lie or refuse disclosure.

- Breaking dynastic control.  
  Pass and enforce a real anti-dynasty law grounded in the Constitution’s intent, so families can't treat public positions as hereditary titles. Disconnect local political power from national contracts, limiting the ability of clans to shield one another.

- Cultural shift around pakikisama.  
  Redefine loyalty so it doesn’t include covering up theft of public money. Encourage community support for whistleblowers rather than labeling them as “walang pakikisama.”

None of these demands are as emotionally satisfying as saying “sana mamatay lahat ng kurakot” or daydreaming about sparrow units in the shadows of EDSA. But they are the difference between a society that vents and a society that rebuilds.

🟥 The heart of the matter

At its core, the Filipino tendency to hate the corrupt instead of insisting on their legal accountability is showing a state of deep desperation. 

Despair that cases will be archived, not decided.  

Despair that dynasties will stay in power despite scandal after scandal.  

Despair that, forty years after Marcos, we are still debating how to punish plunder and recover stolen wealth.

In that despair, violent fantasies become attractive. 

Kara’s birthday wish hits like a release valve. 

The ABB nostalgia feels like an escape hatch. 

Duterte’s old script—kill them first, justify later—still lingers in the national subconscious.

But here’s the risk: every time we normalize wishing death instead of demanding justice, we chip away at whatever is left of our faith in the law. 

We make it easier for the next strongman, the next vigilante, the next “shortcut” to step in and promise blood in place of institutions.

We don’t need more shortcuts. We need institutions that work.

We need courts that the poor can trust and the powerful can fear.  

We need prosecutors who are more afraid of losing the people’s trust than losing a politician’s favor.  

We need laws that are not just written in the statute books but enforced on actual human beings with actual surnames.

So yes, the corrupt deserve to be removed, punished, and permanently kept away from public money. 

They deserve prison, forfeiture of wealth, and lifetime bans from office under the law. 

What they do not need from us is our wish for their death.

Because once we surrender everything to death wishes and nostalgia for death squads, we’re not just giving up on the corrupt.

We’re giving up on ourselves.

141:
There's a particular kind of political amnesia that Filipinos suffer from—one that I've come to accept with equal parts frustration and resignation. 

We forget quickly. 
We forgive easily. 

We get dazzled by a shiny new proposal, especially one that promises to ease our burden on electricity bills, and suddenly all the previous sins fade into background noise. 

This is precisely what makes Chiz Escudero's Senate Bill 476 on VAT exemption for electricity so infuriatingly brilliant.

To be fair to the issue itself, there are real reasons why a VAT cut on electricity sounds attractive to many of us. 

The Philippines has some of the highest power rates in Asia, driven by expensive imported fuel, delayed transmission projects, and profit formulas that allow utilities and the grid operator to earn far more than their counterparts in countries like Thailand and Malaysia. 

On top of that, the 12 percent VAT is slapped on top of already-bloated generation and distribution charges, so even if the Department of Finance insists it’s not “double taxation” in the technical sense, removing it would still shave roughly 10 percent off a typical household bill.

🟥 The policy, on its own

To be fair, the proposal itself is good policy. 

A targeted VAT exemption on electricity sales—not a blanket tax cut, but a surgical intervention on one of the most pressing pain points of our economy—actually makes sense. 

The cascading effect of lower power bills would genuinely help households stretch their pesos and improve industrial competitiveness. 

But context matters here: earlier this month, some proposals were floating around to slash VAT from 12 to 10 percent on everything, and Executive Secretary Ralph Recto shot that down, warning it would wreck government revenues while we're sitting on ₱1.6 trillion in debt. Fair point. 

Escudero's version is narrower—just electricity, not everything—so it does less damage to the budget while still giving relief where people feel it most. 

This is exactly the kind of targeted intervention that good governance should look like.

Lifting VAT on electricity has been a reasonable idea for years; the real problem is who’s now riding it back toward public grace.

🟥 The man behind the proposal

A bill doesn’t walk into the Senate by itself. It always arrives carrying the record of the politician behind it.

Escudero is not coming into this debate as some neutral technocrat trying to help poor families save on their Meralco bill. 

He is coming fresh from a massive flood control scandal that involved billions in budget insertions in the 2025 budget, including around ₱9.1 billion funneled to his bailiwick of Sorsogon. 

This is the same man whose top campaign donor, Lawrence Lubiano of Centerways Construction, saw his company's government contracts balloon from ₱720 million to ₱15.9 billion after gifting Escudero ₱30 million for his 2022 senatorial campaign. 

Over twelvefold. In his bailiwick. 

This is the same man who was ousted as Senate President in September 2025 because the stench of the flood control corruption scandal became too putrid to ignore. 

This is the same man now facing criminal charges filed before the Ombudsman, with the Department of Justice having issued an immigration lookout order against him.

And let's not forget his performance as Senate President during Sara Duterte's impeachment proceedings. 

From February to August 2025, Escudero systematically delayed the trial through calendar manipulation, procedural obstacles, and jurisdictional games—claiming he needed to pass "priority bills" first, violating the Senate's own impeachment rules that require legislative business to be suspended. 

He postponed hearings multiple times, surprised his fellow senators with sudden schedule changes, and eventually had the Senate return the articles to the House with impossible conditions. 

When the Supreme Court finally ruled the complaint unconstitutional in August, he voted to archive the case and gave a long speech about "rule of law"—after spending months blocking the process himself. 

The timing of these delays coincided perfectly with his Senate presidency battle, where Sara's support was politically crucial.

And now—now—he suddenly becomes the champion of cutting electricity costs?

🟥 Image repair disguised as concern

I see what you're doing, Senator. I see the calculation. 

Push a genuinely good bill that benefits ordinary Filipinos, get some positive media coverage, maybe rehabilitate your image before the next election cycle. 

Make people think, "Well, he did steal billions, but at least he cares about my power bill." 

It's a masterclass in political misdirection. It's crime with a compassionate face.

The cruelty of it is that someone else should be pushing this bill. 

Someone whose hands aren't dirty. 

Someone whose motives we don't have to dissect under a microscope because they haven't spent the last two years funneling government money to their provinces and their contractors. 

Someone who hasn't been ousted from the highest position in the Senate for corruption.

But no. Escudero gets to introduce it. 

Escudero gets the headlines. 

Escudero gets to frame himself as the guy who cares about Filipino families struggling with power bills.

🟥 The real problem

This is the problem with our system. And this is why I write about these things over my morning coffee.

Filipinos are forgetful—I know this, you know this, Chiz Escudero absolutely knows this. 

We move on. We get distracted by the next scandal. 

By next year, maybe the criminal cases fade. 

Maybe he wins his next election anyway. 

Maybe this VAT exemption becomes law and people remember him fondly for saving them money on electricity, and forget entirely about the ₱15.9 billion in flood control contracts that went to his buddies, or the ₱9.1 billion in mysterious budget insertions.

I won't forget. 

And I'll keep saying it, because someone needs to keep floating this sin where Filipinos can see it.

Yes, the policy is good. But the person pushing it has spent the last few years proving exactly why he shouldn't be trusted with anything, least of all the peoples' money.

Support the bill if it passes. Call it good policy. 

But don't let Chiz Escudero rebrand himself as a champion of the people. 

He earned his reputation for legal corruption the hard way. Let him keep it.

Still bitter about the coffee, and about a lot of other things.

[MCT | Commentary]

James Deakin's son, Daniel's traffic violation case has become a flashpoint for a much bigger conversation.

WARNING: LONG PIECE AHEAD.

Now, a disclaimer: before you accuse me of riding on the issue just like everyone else, understand this.

Just like James, my wife and I taught our daughter how to drive, and she had to go through what Daniel went through to get her license.

I also taught my daughter that laws and rules are important, and that behind those are procedures, processes, bureaucracy, red tape, and a lot of unnecessary back and forth.

I may not be a road safety advocate the way James is, but I consider myself a good governance advocate, and you cannot talk about what’s right if you do not also advocate road safety and discipline.

More importantly, this issue is multilayered, and it’s worth looking into, because if you own and operate a vehicle in the Philippines and you are licensed, this issue affects you.

Going back, this case involves more than a teenager crossing a double yellow line. The real issue here is bureaucracy, power, accountability, and the gap between rules on paper and how they actually work when you need them.

🟥 What Actually Happened

December 18, 2025. Daniel Deakin, 19 years old, is driving on Skyway Stage 3. 

He's a new driver, got his license just months before. 

His father, James Deakin, made him do it the hard way: no fixers, no shortcuts, all legitimate. 

Classroom theory. Practical driving course. The whole LTO licensing process, by the book.

Daniel doesn't know the Skyway layout well yet. 

He realizes too late he needs the Quezon Avenue exit. 

In that split-second panic, he crosses a double solid yellow line. 

A traffic enforcer pulls him over.

The ticket says "reckless driving."

Not "improper lane change." Not "lane violation." Reckless driving.

Daniel calls his dad, shaken. 

James tells him to take responsibility, pay the fine, move on. 

That's what following the rules looks like.

Then they try to settle the violation. And that's where things get messy.

🟥 The LTO Nightmare (James Deakin's Words)

The ticket says they have 15 days to settle and get Daniel's license back. It's a few days before Christmas. James and Daniel plan to handle it during the break between Christmas and New Year.

They show up at the LTO office on a Saturday. It's closed.

They check online. The schedule says it should be open for license retrieval. But it's not. They go back on January 5, the first chance they get after the holidays.

After hours of being shuffled from one window to another, they pay the ₱2,000 fine. 

But the LTO won't release Daniel's license. 

They want photocopies of the vehicle's Official Receipt and Certificate of Registration (OR/CR).

James tries to explain: the car isn't theirs. All the vehicle information is already on the traffic ticket, plate number, make, model, registered owner. Everything the LTO would need is right there.

"No. We need the printed OR/CR. And we need a photocopy."

They come back with the documents. From the manufacturer. Because the vehicle wasn't fully registered yet, it was in that gray zone between dealer and buyer.

Too late, the LTO says. The 15-day period has lapsed. Daniel's license is now suspended for a month.

"But you were closed. Christmas. New Year. Rizal Day."

Doesn't matter. Calendar days, not business days.

James Deakin, a road safety advocate and automotive journalist with years of experience calling out bad drivers and pushing for better traffic discipline, loses it. 

Not because his son got a ticket. But because of what the system just taught his son.

That following the rules is for chumps. That the fixer culture exists because the LTO makes it almost impossible to do things the right way.

He posts everything on Facebook. It goes viral.

🟥 What the LTO Says

On January 7, 2026, LTO Chief Markus Lacanilao holds a press briefing. He doesn't back down. He doubles down.

Two violations, not one.

First: Daniel crossed a double solid line. That's a traffic violation, yes. 

But Lacanilao shows CCTV footage, the pickup truck swerves across lanes, cutting in front of vehicles trying to exit, causing traffic to slow down behind him. More than a  lane mistake, that's dangerous driving.

Second, and this is where it gets legally murky: the vehicle was unregistered.

Daniel didn't have an OR/CR. He had a sales invoice, but not from the dealer to the buyer. 

He had the invoice from the importer to the dealer. 

Under LTO rules, that means the vehicle hadn't been sold yet. It wasn't supposed to be on public roads.

"Ang pagmamaneho ng sasakyan na hindi rehistrado ay considered reckless driving," Lacanilao says. Driving an unregistered vehicle is, by definition, reckless driving.

So both violations, crossing the double line and driving an unregistered vehicle, together justify the "reckless driving" classification.

On the timeline issue, Lacanilao is firm. The LTO policy is clear: 15 calendar days, not business days. 

Daniel was apprehended on December 18. He had until January 2 to settle. That includes weekends. That includes holidays. That's how the rule works.

"Given that the apprehension took place during the holiday season, it is anticipated that certain government offices would be closed. 

Therefore, motorists should consider existing holidays and office hours when fulfilling their responsibilities within the designated timeframe," Lacanilao says.

Daniel also had five days to contest the violation, until December 23. He didn't file an appeal. 

By LTO logic, the violation was deemed admitted when they paid the fine without contesting.

But the LTO isn't just blaming Daniel. They've issued show-cause orders:

- One for Daniel, requiring him to appear at a hearing on January 12 to explain why he shouldn't be held liable for reckless driving  

- One for the car dealer, to explain why they released a vehicle without proper registration documents. The dealer could lose their LTO accreditation  

- One for the traffic enforcer, summoning him to the same hearing to explain his side  

Lacanilao also clarifies something important: reckless driving, under the traffic code, is not a criminal offense. It's an administrative violation. It only becomes "reckless imprudence", a criminal charge, if it results in injury, death, or property damage.

🟥 The Legal Gray Areas

This is the part where things start getting messy. 

What does the law really say here?

■ What is "Reckless Driving" in the Philippines?

Under Republic Act 4136 (the Land Transportation and Traffic Code), reckless driving is defined as operating a motor vehicle "without reasonable caution," considering traffic conditions, road conditions, visibility, and in a way that endangers the safety or property of others.

The definition is deliberately broad. It's not just speeding or drunk driving. It includes:

- Counterflowing  
- Beating red lights  
- Weaving dangerously between lanes  
- Tailgating at high speed  
- Overtaking in prohibited zones  
- Driving with defective brakes or lights  
- Driving an unregistered vehicle  

That last one is key. The LTO argues that operating an unregistered vehicle is inherently reckless because the vehicle hasn't been certified as roadworthy.

The penalty for reckless driving:  
- First offense: ₱2,000 fine  
- Second offense: ₱3,000 fine + 3-month license suspension  
- Third offense: ₱10,000 fine + 6-month suspension or license revocation  

Daniel got the first-offense penalty: ₱2,000 and a one-month suspension.

■ The Unregistered Vehicle Issue

Under RA 4136 Section 5, you cannot operate a motor vehicle on any public highway without current registration. The penalty: ₱10,000 fine and possible impoundment if the vehicle is unregistered for more than a month.

The problem here is who knew what, and when.

Did Daniel know the vehicle lacked proper registration? Did the dealer tell him? Did his father know?

The LTO says the papers Daniel presented, the importer-to-dealer invoice, make it clear the car "hadn't been sold yet" and shouldn't have been on the road. 

The dealer is now facing a show-cause order that could cost them their accreditation.

But here's the thing: most drivers assume that if a dealer hands them keys and lets them drive off the lot, the vehicle is legal to drive. Temporary permits. In-transit registrations. There are mechanisms for this. The question is whether those mechanisms were used, or ignored.

■ Calendar Days vs. Business Days

This is the kind of bureaucratic move that people feel in real life.

LTO policy, per Memorandum Circular 515-2004, gives motorists 15 calendar days to settle a violation. Not 15 business days. Calendar days. That means weekends and holidays count.

Daniel was apprehended on December 18. His 15 days ran from December 19 to January 2.

During that period:  
- December 21–22: Weekend  
- December 24–26: Christmas (government offices closed)  
- December 28–29: Weekend  
- December 30: Rizal Day (holiday)  
- December 31: New Year's Eve (offices closed early or entirely)  
- January 1: New Year's Day (holiday)  

That's at least 10 days when the LTO was either closed or operating on reduced hours.

James Deakin's argument: How can the government give you 15 days to settle a violation but then close its offices for more than half that time?

The LTO's response: That's the rule. It's the same across all government agencies. Motorists should plan around it.

One reporter asked Lacanilao at the briefing whether the LTO would consider changing the policy to count only business days. He said they'd "look into it." But as of now, the rule stands.

🟥 Did Daniel Have Five Days to Contest?

The LTO also says Daniel had five days from the date of apprehension, until December 23, to contest the violation. He didn't. So when he paid the fine, the violation was deemed admitted.

But December 23 is two days before Christmas. And based on James's account, they were still trying to process what had happened, traveling, dealing with the holidays. The five-day window to contest is short in the best of circumstances. During the holiday season, it's brutal.

🟥 The Dealer's Role

One thing that quietly sits in the background is this: why did the dealer even let Daniel drive a car that wasn’t properly registered?

New cars go through a process:  
1. Importer brings car into the Philippines  
2. Dealer buys from importer (invoice from importer to dealer)  
3. Dealer sells to buyer (invoice from dealer to buyer, plus OR/CR)  
4. Buyer registers vehicle with LTO  

Daniel had the importer-to-dealer document. Not the dealer-to-buyer document. Not the OR/CR.

That suggests the vehicle was still in dealer inventory. It hadn't completed the sale process. It shouldn't have been on the road.

Car dealers sometimes let buyers "test" vehicles or drive them off the lot before all paperwork is finalized, especially for high-value clients. But that's supposed to be done with temporary permits or dealer plates. If the dealer skipped that step, they're in serious trouble.

Lacanilao was blunt: "Such practice by car dealers is wrong. They may lose their accreditation if they are doing that."

The dealer is now facing a show-cause order. We don't know yet what the outcome will be. But if the LTO revokes their accreditation, that's a business-ending penalty.

🟥 The Traffic Enforcer's Discretion

The enforcer who issued the ticket classified the violation as "reckless driving" on the spot.

Was that appropriate? Did he have the authority to make that call?

Traffic enforcers have discretion in how they classify violations. A lane change can be "improper lane change" (₱500–₱1,000 fine) or "reckless driving" (₱2,000 fine plus potential suspension), depending on the circumstances.

The LTO says the CCTV footage backs up the enforcer's judgment, the lane change was sudden, disruptive, and caused traffic to slow. Combined with the unregistered vehicle, "reckless driving" was justified.

But here's the tension: the enforcer didn't know about the unregistered vehicle until after he stopped Daniel. So the initial classification was based solely on the lane violation.

Did the enforcer upgrade the charge once he saw the paperwork? Or was "reckless driving" the label from the start?

The LTO has summoned the enforcer to the January 12 hearing to explain, which suggests even the LTO wants clarity on this.

🟥 The Father: James Deakin

James Deakin is not just any concerned parent. He's a road safety advocate. He's been on TV, on CNN Philippines, writing columns, making videos. He's spent years telling Filipinos to follow the rules, drive defensively, respect traffic laws.

He's called out reckless drivers. He's pushed for stricter enforcement. He's advocated for dash cams, better driver education, accountability.

And now he's on the other side of the system he's been defending.

Some people see this as hypocrisy. Or irony. Or karma.

But James isn't denying his son made a mistake. He's not saying Daniel shouldn't have been ticketed. He's saying the punishment doesn't fit the violation, and the process is designed to fail.

His complaint is specific:

1. "Reckless driving" is too severe for a lane violation by a new driver who made a slow, cautious mistake  

2. The LTO demanded documents (OR/CR) that added no enforcement value, all vehicle information was already on the ticket  

3. Counting holidays as part of the 15-day settlement window is absurd when the LTO is closed during those days  

4. The system creates so much friction that it encourages people to use fixers instead of following the rules  

That last point is the sharpest. James tried to teach his son that following the rules is how societies improve. The LTO taught his son the opposite.

On January 7, after the LTO briefing, James attended a Zoom hearing with the Anti-Red Tape Authority (ARTA). He was more positive about that experience:

"My biggest concern through all this was teaching my son that rules matter, and that we improve as a society by following them. The LTO let me down badly, and it cut deep, but at least now I can tell him there's accountability even when government agencies don't play by the rules. That means something."

🟥 The Media and Public Reaction

The story exploded. News crews showed up at James Deakin's house. Every major outlet covered it: GMA, ABS-CBN, Inquirer, Philstar, Manila Standard.

Why? Because it hit a nerve.

Millions of Filipinos have had nightmare experiences with the LTO. Long lines. Arbitrary requirements. Missing license plates they paid for years ago. Fixers lurking outside, offering to "help" for a fee.

James's post goes beyond his son and speaks to every motorist who's been told the rules, tried to follow them, and gotten punished anyway.

The public reaction split into camps:

Camp 1: "James is right. The LTO is broken."

These are the people who've dealt with calendar-day rules during holidays, been asked for irrelevant documents, or waited hours only to be told they're missing one more piece of paper. They see James as finally saying what they've been thinking.

Camp 2: "Rules are rules. Daniel broke two laws."

These are the people who point out that Daniel did cross a double line, was driving an unregistered vehicle, and didn't contest the violation within five days. The LTO followed its policy. Daniel (or his father) should have planned better.

Camp 3: "This is what privilege looks like."

Some argue that James, as a well-known influencer with media access and an audience, gets to make noise in a way that ordinary motorists can't. His viral post triggered an ARTA hearing and national media attention. Would Daniel have gotten the same treatment if his father wasn't famous?

Camp 4: "Everyone is at fault here."

The son made a mistake. The father should have checked the vehicle registration before letting his son drive it. The dealer shouldn't have released an unregistered vehicle. The LTO's calendar-day policy is harsh. The traffic enforcer maybe overclassified the violation. And the media is capitalizing on the controversy for clicks.

🟥 What the Public Doesn't Always See

There's a bigger context here that gets lost in the outrage.

🟥 Fixer Culture is Real—and the LTO Knows It

The LTO has been fighting fixers for years. In 2024, then-Assistant Secretary Vigor Mendoza issued a memo reminding field offices to "prohibit fixers from entering, roaming, waiting" in LTO facilities. In 2025, the LTO fired 68 enforcers from its Central Office for corruption.

But fixer culture exists because the official process is so frustrating. Long lines. Confusing requirements. Arbitrary enforcement. Offices closed when you need them. Fixers promise to solve all of that, for a price.

When the system is easier to bypass than to follow, bypass becomes the rational choice.

James Deakin's point is that the LTO creates demand for fixers by making the legitimate process unnecessarily difficult.

🟥 The LTO is Also Overwhelmed

The LTO has been dealing with a backlog of millions of license plates for years. They've promised to clear it by various deadlines. They haven't. Motorists are driving without plates, plates the LTO was supposed to issue, and then getting ticketed for not having plates.

The agency is trying to digitalize. They've launched the Land Transport Management System (LTMS) to streamline processes. But rollout has been uneven. Some branches are fully digital. Others still rely on paper forms.

The LTO is also dealing with fraud, corruption, and enforcement gaps. They're trying to clean house. But institutional reform takes time.

None of this excuses the calendar-day policy or the bureaucratic friction. But it does explain why the LTO can sometimes feel like it's at war with the public it's supposed to serve.

🟥 New Drivers Make Mistakes. That's Why They're Called New Drivers.

Daniel had his license for a few months. He's 19. He didn't know the Skyway layout. He panicked and crossed a line he shouldn't have.

That's what new drivers do. They make slow, cautious mistakes. They overcorrect. They misjudge distances. They don't know the roads yet.

Is that "reckless driving"? Or is it just learning to drive?

The LTO would say: it doesn't matter. The law prohibits crossing double solid lines. Ignorance isn't an excuse. If you cause a traffic disruption, that's reckless.

But here's the tension: if every new-driver mistake gets classified as "reckless driving," then the label loses meaning. "Reckless" implies willful disregard for safety. It implies intent. A teenager unfamiliar with an exit and making a clumsy lane change doesn't feel like that.

🟥 Where the Faults Really Lie

To be fair, halos lahat may sabit dito, in different ways.

■ Daniel's Fault

He crossed a double solid yellow line. That's a violation. Period. He also drove a vehicle that wasn't properly registered, though it's unclear whether he knew that.

Accountability level: Real, but proportional.

■ James Deakin's Fault

He let his son drive a vehicle without verifying that the registration was complete. As a road safety advocate, he should have known to check the OR/CR. He also didn't contest the violation within the five-day window.

Accountability level: Shared responsibility as a parent and vehicle supervisor.

■ The Dealer's Fault

They released a vehicle without proper registration documents. If they let Daniel drive it without a temporary permit or in-transit registration, that's a serious violation. They're facing a show-cause order and potential loss of accreditation.

Accountability level: High. This is their job. They should have known better.

■ The Traffic Enforcer's Potential Fault

Did he overclassify the violation as "reckless driving" when it should have been "improper lane change"? The LTO says CCTV justifies the label. But the enforcer is being summoned to explain, which suggests the LTO wants to make sure he followed protocol.

Accountability level: Under investigation.

■ The LTO's Fault

The calendar-day policy is harsh. Counting days when the agency itself is closed feels like entrapment. The demand for printed OR/CR when all vehicle information was already on the ticket is bureaucratic friction with no clear enforcement value. And the general culture of red tape, long waits, and missing services (like license plates) erodes public trust.

Accountability level: Systemic. The rules may be "correct" on paper, but they're poorly designed for real-world use.

■ The Media's Role

Is this news? Yes. Is it important? Yes. But is it also being milked for clicks and engagement? Also yes. 

The story plays into a narrative: ordinary citizen vs. broken system. Influencer uses platform to fight back. That's compelling television. But it also risks sensationalizing a situation that's more complicated than "good guy vs. bad agency."

Accountability level: Doing their job, but maybe with too much spectacle.

■ The Public Jumping to Conclusions

A lot of people sided with James immediately, before the LTO presented their side. That's natural, people distrust the LTO, and James is likeable and articulate. But the rush to judgment, the hashtags, the pile-on, all happened before anyone knew about the unregistered vehicle.

Accountability level: Normal human behavior, but worth noting.

🟥 What This Case Reveals About the System

If you set aside the personalities and the drama, this case shows us a few uncomfortable truths:

1. The rules are technically being followed, but they don't serve the people they're supposed to protect. Calendar days that include government-closed holidays. Document requirements that add no enforcement value. Short contest windows during the busiest travel season of the year. The system is designed for compliance, not for fairness.

2. Dealer practices need more scrutiny. If dealers are releasing unregistered vehicles to buyers, that's a bigger problem than one traffic ticket. The LTO's show-cause order to the dealer might be the most important outcome of this case.

3. "Reckless driving" as a catch-all label creates confusion and resentment. If the term covers everything from drunk driving to crossing a double line in an unregistered vehicle to tailgating, then it stops meaning anything specific. People need to understand the severity of their violations. Right now, "reckless driving" sounds criminal even when it's not.

4. Fixer culture thrives when the legitimate process is too painful. Every unnecessary hoop, every closed office during a settlement window, every arbitrary requirement is an advertisement for fixers.

5. Influencer privilege is real, but so is influencer accountability. James Deakin got an ARTA hearing and national attention because of who he is. Most motorists won't. But James also used his platform to raise an issue that affects millions. That's what platforms are for. The question is whether the system changes for everyone, or just for people who can go viral.

🟥 What Happens Next

The January 12 hearing at LTO Central Office will be critical. Daniel, the traffic enforcer, and presumably the dealer will all appear to explain their sides.

ARTA is investigating whether the LTO's processes violated anti-red tape rules. If they find the calendar-day policy or the document requirements are unjustified barriers, the LTO may be forced to change them.

The dealer could lose their accreditation if the LTO finds they knowingly released an unregistered vehicle.

Daniel's one-month suspension stands, unless the hearing overturns it.

And James Deakin? He's already said his biggest concern was teaching his son that rules matter. Whether the LTO changes or not, that lesson got a lot more complicated.

🟥 The Bigger Question

I keep circling back to this one thing. This story went viral because it's not really about James Deakin or his son. 

James's post goes beyond his son and speaks to every motorist who's been told the rules, tried to follow them, and gotten punished anyway.

Deep down, this whole thing is really about the unspoken deal between people and their government. 

Citizens agree to follow the rules. 
Government agrees to make those rules fair, clear, and enforceable. 

When one side breaks the contract, the whole system starts to crack.

The LTO says: We're following the rules. Calendar days. Proper documentation. Reckless driving classification. It's all by the book.

And maybe they are.

But if following the rules feels like punishment, if the system is easier to bypass than to obey, if new drivers making slow mistakes get the same label as drunk drivers or street racers, then something is broken.

Not just in the LTO. In the way we think about accountability. In the way we design systems. In the gap between what the law says and what justice feels like.

James Deakin wanted to teach his son that following the rules is how societies improve. The LTO taught his son something else. Maybe not the lesson they intended. But the lesson he, and millions of other Filipinos, learned anyway.

That the rules only work if the system behind them does. And right now, the system doesn't.

To James and Daniel and to the LTO,

I just woke up and the first thing I see is this woman's face and a statement from her that immediately rang alarms in my head. 

To me, Imee Marcos’ mouth is dangerous. Para itong si Sara Duterte na kapag nag bukas ng bibig, someone has to fight the resulting fire somewhere.  

So let’s fact-check this woman before the gullible ones jump in to believe her.

While commenting on the US operation in Venezuela that captured President Nicolas Maduro, she said: “This sounds personal to me because of what happened in 1986. It is my belief that the US kidnapped my father and our family when we were transported out.”

Kidnapped.

That’s the word she used. The same word her father Ferdinand Marcos Sr. used back in 1987 when he claimed he was “deceived and practically kidnaped out of the Philippines.”

But was it kidnapping? Or is this another attempt to rewrite what actually happened during those final hours in Malacañang?

Let’s look at what the records show.

🟥 What Actually Happened in February 1986

The People Power Revolution was at its peak. Millions of Filipinos had taken to the streets. Military defections were mounting. Defense Minister Juan Ponce Enrile and General Fidel Ramos had joined the opposition. The palace was about to be stormed.

And Ferdinand Marcos? He was making phone calls.

At around 3 a.m. Manila time on February 25, 1986, Marcos called Senator Paul Laxalt, a close friend of US President Ronald Reagan. Marcos asked if Reagan wanted him to step down. He floated the idea of staying until 1987, or perhaps a power-sharing arrangement with Corazon Aquino.

Laxalt consulted with Reagan and called back with a message: “I think you should cut, and cut cleanly. I think the time has come.”

Marcos responded: “I’m so very, very disappointed.”

But here’s the thing: Marcos also asked for help. He spoke to Enrile, asking for safe passage for him, his family, and his close allies including General Fabian Ver. Reagan offered sanctuary in the United States.

The US helicopters that picked up Marcos and his party did not appear out of nowhere. 

The request for those choppers came through US Ambassador Stephen Bosworth after Washington decided that Marcos had to be taken out to avoid a bloodbath.

By the evening of February 25, four Jolly Green Giant helicopters landed at Malacañang. 

They carried Marcos, Imelda, their children, General Ver, and around 80 to 89 other relatives and cronies to Clark Air Base. 

There was no firefight, no shootout over the choppers, no commando-style snatch. 

It was a negotiated extraction for a dictator on the way out.

That last part is important. The plan had to be accepted at Malacañang. You do not accept a kidnapping. You endure it.

This is not what kidnapping looks like.

🟥 The Destination Dispute

Here’s where the Marcos family’s “kidnapped” narrative finds its only small foothold: they wanted to go to Ilocos Norte, not Hawaii.

At Clark Air Base, Marcos asked to be flown to his home province. He wanted to stay somewhere in the Philippines, under security, near his base, with a chance to regroup or at least die on home soil.

Reagan’s diary entry for February 25, 1986, is clear that the US tried to get Aquino’s consent for Marcos to stay in the country with security guarantees. 

He wrote that they ordered their Ambassador and others to contact Aquino and try to persuade her to accept Marcos staying in the islands with promises of protection.

Aquino refused. 

She vetoed any plan to let him remain in the Philippines. She wanted Marcos out of the country completely. 

Reagan was reportedly unhappy with that refusal, but the decision was hers, and the US followed it.

So yes, Marcos was denied his preferred destination. He wanted Ilocos Norte. He got Guam, then Honolulu.

But that is not kidnapping. That is being denied a choice of where to go in exile after you have lost power and requested safe passage out.

🟥 What Marcos Brought With Him

Let’s talk about what Marcos brought on this alleged “kidnapping.”

This was not a rushed escape with nothing but the clothes on their backs. When the Marcos party left, they carried:

- Crates of freshly minted Philippine pesos, including millions in cash.  
- Gold bars.  
- Hundreds of pieces of jewelry.  
- Deeds, bank certificates, and documents tied to their wealth.

Later investigations and appraisals would put some numbers on it. US Customs in Hawaii seized jewelry valued at around half a million dollars. 

Philippine and international reporting would later estimate that the baggage and assets they brought in that exile flight ran into the millions of dollars, with one report putting their party’s baggage value at around $7.7 million.

Bongbong Marcos would later stand in front of an audience in Hawaii and say their family arrived with “nothing” and survived only because Filipinos there helped them. 

That story is emotionally useful, but it is not true. 

The records in Hawaii and the official appraisals do not support the “we arrived empty-handed” claim.

When you are kidnapped, you usually do not have time to pack crates of money, gold, luxury jewelry, and piles of documents.

🟥 How Courts and Historians Describe It

Courts in Hawaii, summarizing the events, wrote that Marcos and his family fled the Philippines for Hawaii in February 1986 in the wake of political unrest after he was deposed.

Fled. Not kidnapped.

Political science and historical work describe it the same way. 

The standard language is that the United States flew an ousted Marcos to exile in Hawaii to avoid further bloodshed after a fraudulent election and a mass uprising.

Academic and historical accounts use words like fled, exile, forced to flee, ouster. These are not words you use for someone who has been kidnapped. These are words for someone who has lost power and left under pressure.

Even sympathetic or neutral accounts that repeat Marcos’ own line will usually make a distinction between what he claimed and what the evidence shows. 

At most, they highlight that he felt deceived about the final destination, not that American soldiers stormed the palace and grabbed him against his will.

🟥 The Pattern

This is not the first time Imee has tried to retell what happened to her family in a more flattering way.

She has told people to “move on” from the abuses, killings, and disappearances during her father’s dictatorship. 

When asked about apologizing, she said she was open to it but would never admit guilt. 

The Marcos formula is familiar by now: admit nothing, express vague regret, then quickly pivot to their own supposed suffering.

Even in this latest statement, there is a small trick inside the language. 

She says, “It is my belief that the US kidnapped my father and our family.” 

Framing it as belief makes it sound personal and untouchable, as if you are not allowed to question it because it is “just her truth.” 

But belief is not a shield from facts. You can sincerely believe something that is still wrong.

So belief or not, a claim like that still has real political impact. 

It still shapes how young people understand 1986, People Power, and the role of the United States. 

It still has to be fact-checked. Personally, I would not buy anything she is selling me, and I refuse to treat “belief” as a free pass from evidence.

The historical record exists. The phone calls, the diaries, the government documents, the eyewitness accounts, the court decisions. They paint a clear picture.

Ferdinand Marcos was not kidnapped in 1986. He lost power. He asked for help getting out safely. The United States provided that help. There was a dispute about where he would end up, and he did not get his way.

That is not kidnapping. Plain and simple, that is exile.

And when Imee Marcos says her family was kidnapped, she is not just sharing a personal memory. She is trying to shift the story, blur the lines between victim and culprit, and put her powerful family back in the role of the wronged.

Some people will accept that. Some are tired, busy, or just want less conflict in their feeds.

But some of us remember.

The Marcoses left on US helicopters because the alternative was facing millions of Filipinos who had filled the streets and were done with being afraid. They left with wealth they could not explain. They left because their time was up.

There is a difference between being kidnapped and being forced to deal with the consequences of your actions.

Imee knows the difference. She just hopes you do not.

If you ask me, the Marcoses got lucky that night. If not for the intervention of the US, they could have ended up like Mussolini, Gaddafi, or Villarroel, killed and their bodies put on display by their own people.

144:
Marcos’ numbers are not falling by accident.  

They are falling because people are tired. And sitting here, reading survey after survey, ang tanong ko na lang sa sarili ko is: kung fan ka ng presidenteng ito paano mo pa siya ipagtatanggol?

🟥 Looking At Surveys and Public Opinion

There was a time I tried to give him the benefit of the doubt. 

Early days pa lang ng term niya, surveys said roughly three out of four Filipinos were satisfied with his performance. 

Honeymoon phase, fine. 

People were hopeful. Some of my own friends were like, “Baka naman he’ll be better than the father.”

Then I watch the numbers slide.  

By around early 2024, Pulse Asia was already showing his approval dropping from the high 70s to the mid‑50s, with disapproval doubling. 

Hindi ito chismis sa kanto. These are millions of people quietly changing their mind.

By the end of 2025, halos kalahati na ng adult Filipinos were saying they disapproved of his performance. 

SWS had his net trust at negative three, meaning mas marami na ang hindi nagtitiwala kaysa nagtitiwala. 

When I read that, I caught myself thinking: “So it’s not just me. Hindi ako nag-iinarte lang.”

When I look at those numbers, I don’t see politics. Nakikita ko mga taong gaya ko na quietly umaatras.

He started with all that goodwill. Now he’s underwater. Hindi siya inabot doon nang walang dahilan.

🟥 Ano ba talaga kinagagalit ng tao?

Whenever I check the surveys, I notice something very simple.  

Wala namang masyadong arte yung hinihingi ng mga Pilipino. The top concerns keep repeating: inflation, corruption, low wages, poverty, jobs.

I think of friends na kalahati ng sahod napupunta sa groceries, of parents na hindi makapag-enroll ng anak sa private kahit gusto nila. I look at my own budget and feel that slow squeeze sa presyo ng pagkain at kuryente.

And then sa balita, what do we see?

- Corruption stories around big-ticket projects and agencies.  
- Familiar names popping up around these projects.  
- “Investigations” na parang pampabango lang, mabagal, selective.  
- A president who seems more irritated with critics than with the corruption itself.

Analysts say corruption has now climbed right behind high prices as a top concern. I do not need an analyst to tell me that. I can feel it in conversations, in the “hay naku” sa group chats, in the way people roll their eyes when government is mentioned.

So when I see his ratings drop, hindi ako nagtataka.  

It feels like the country is saying out loud what a lot of us have been thinking quietly: pagod na kami.

🟥 The cabinet reshuffle that feels fake

Then came the dramatic move: courtesy resignations, cabinet “reset,” post‑midterms drama. For a second, I wanted to believe. Maybe, I thought, this is the turning point.

But when I looked closely, ang dami palang na-retain.

- Economic team mostly stayed.  
- Loyal allies stayed.  
- Some were just moved from one post to another, parang musical chairs.  
- A few were replaced, then recycled into other roles.

As a writer, I care about patterns. And the pattern I see is this: ang lakas sa gesture, ang hina sa substance.

Commentators were already warning that if policies do not change, the reshuffle is just image work. And honestly, that’s how it felt to me. Parang nilipat-lipat lang yung upuan sa lumulubog na barko.

Sabi ko nga sa post kahapon: nasusunog yung bahay mo pero ang naiisip mong solusyon is to rearrange the furniture in the living room. 

Seriously,  WTF, right? 🤷‍♂️

He treats it like a staffing problem.  

We are living a policy problem.

🟥 My main problem: half-baked siya

This is where I really lose patience with him.  

My problem with Marcos is his actions are always half-baked. Laging bitin. Laging safe.

The clearest example for me is the whole mess with unprogrammed funds.

For 2025 and 2026, Congress loaded the budget with massive “unprogrammed appropriations” — hundreds of billions in standby funds. Matagal na itong binabatikos, kasi these pots are super flexible and super prone to pork and ghost projects.

Then, late 2025, a Supreme Court justice basically says what a lot of us have been thinking. 

Justice Ramon Paul Hernando calls unprogrammed appropriations, in any form, unconstitutional — riders with no clear funding sources, breeding grounds for corruption. 

After that, lawmakers and lawyers start talking about challenging them before the Court.

So what does Marcos do?

- He signs the 2025 budget but vetoes around P194 billion in items, including some unprogrammed funds.  
- He signs the 2026 budget, then vetoes P92.5 billion in unprogrammed items, reportedly seven out of ten unprogrammed lines.  
- Then he goes out and acts like this proves his commitment to transparency and fiscal discipline.

And here’s where I just stare at the news and mutter to myself:  

So alam mong may constitutional issue, pero nagtira ka pa?

If you truly believe these unprogrammed funds are constitutionally problematic, bakit hindi mo ni‑veto lahat? If the entire structure is sick, why are you so proud you removed just a slice and left the rest?

Makabayan called his P92.5‑billion veto a “token gesture,” pointing out that plenty of unprogrammed items remain in the 2026 budget and that big, graft‑prone items were spared. 

Analysts noted the same thing earlier: even after his 2025 veto, pork and opaque funds remained huge in key agencies like DPWH.

As a citizen, it makes me feel insulted.  

He wants applause for doing the bare minimum.

Kung mali, alisin mo.  
Kung tama, ipaglaban mo.  

Ang ginawa niya: admit na may problema, then keep just enough of it to stay politically comfortable. That is the pattern that keeps bothering me.

🟥 Token gestures, real damage

On paper, the numbers look big: P194 billion vetoed here, P92.5 billion there. Malacañang talks about transparency, some senators praise the veto. But when I zoom out, what I see is a familiar script:

- The unprogrammed framework survives.  
- Pork and flexible funds survive.  
- The Supreme Court still has to clean up what Malacañang refused to fully confront.

Ordinary people do not read veto messages and concurring opinions.  

But we feel patterns.

We see a president who:

- Boasts about vetoing billions that probably should not have been there in the first place.  
- Flirts with the idea that these funds are constitutionally questionable.  
- Still signs budgets that keep a chunk of those same funds intact.

That’s when I realized:  

This is a deliberate choice to do just enough to claim credit, but not enough to fix the system.

Half-baked. All the time.

Masabi lang na may ginagawa. 

🟥 The thing he won’t say out loud

Kung titingnan mo, consistent siya sa isang bagay: ayaw niyang umamin.

If midterms go badly, he looks at his cabinet.  
If surveys sink, he talks about misinformation or critics.  

If corruption stories explode, he talks about “investigations” but rarely burns bridges with powerful allies.

By the end of 2025, surveys painted a clear picture:

- His disapproval rising to around the high 40s.  
- His approval shrinking to the low to mid‑30s.  
- His net trust sitting in the negatives, while other big names still post much higher positive trust.

You don’t need to be a fan of any of those other politicians to understand what people are saying here. They’re comparing energy, decisiveness, and backbone. And in that comparison, Marcos looks weak.

As someone who writes about politics, I find myself repeating one question:  

Saan ka ba talaga willing makipagsabong, Mr. President?

Because right now, it looks like you’ll fight for optics, photo ops, and partial vetoes. But when it comes to fully cleaning up budgets, cutting off corrupt allies, or admitting you got something wrong, biglang wala.

🟥 Why this keeps me up at night

Ito yung nakakainis: some people say, “Wala namang re‑election, tapusin na lang natin six years, tiis na lang.”  

Pero hindi ganun kasimple.

When a president’s trust collapses:

- Every new policy is questioned, even the good ones.  
- Anti‑corruption efforts look selective and insincere.  
- International partners sense weakness and push harder in negotiations.  
- Local politicians start hedging and playing their own games.

And underneath all of that, there’s this quiet fear I feel from people:  

“If we can’t even trust him to be decisive and honest about money, what happens when the next big crisis hits?”

Another pandemic. A regional conflict. A food crisis. A climate disaster.  
Pumili ka. Or lahat sabay-sabay.

Low trust at the top makes every disaster worse. And that’s why these half-baked decisions are not just annoying to me as a writer and a Filipino. They’re dangerous.

🟥 What I wish he’d do (but doubt he will)

Kung sapilitan akong maging hopeful, ito yung gusto kong makita:

- A real, public break with compromised allies, not just quiet reassignments.  
- Clear, measurable targets on inflation and wages, with regular honest updates, not spin.  
- Full honesty about mistakes: “Nagkamali ako. Babaguhin natin.”  
- A decision to kill unprogrammed appropriations as a class, not just trim them, and to force Congress to legislate a clean replacement.  
- Less travel, more visible work and presence in moments of crisis.

None of these are impossible.  

But they all require something he has not shown so far: the willingness to lose something — an ally, some pork, a bit of political comfort — in order to do the right thing.

So yes, his approval is sliding. His trust rating is negative. People are tired, angry, and over it.  

And here I am, staring at another cabinet reshuffle, another partial veto, another speech, thinking out loud:

The problem is not the surveys, or the cabinet, or even just the budget line items.

The problem is a president who is always half-baked when the country needs someone fully in.

Sabi ko dati, Duterte set the bar so low that he practically made Marcos look like a hero. 

Looking back at that old line now, ang kaya ko na lang sabihin: sige, fine, maybe the bar moved a few inches higher under him, but that’s it, doon lang umangat. 

There are only a few years left before 2028 and he is term‑limited, and unless something close to a miracle happens, he is not going to turn into the kind of president this country actually needs. 

The way things are going, those last two years of his term will not be a season of courage or clarity, just a long, awkward stretch where everyone can see it already: he is nothing more than a sitting duck waiting to be kicked out.

Disclaimer: 

This piece is not a call for Marcos to resign. Kung tutuusin, mas kadiri at mas delikado pa yung mga pwedeng ipalit sa kanya sa ngayon. 

This is a critique, an opinion piece, grounded in how I feel as a citizen and a writer, and in the data and reports I have been reading. 

It is frustration on paper, not a roadmap for ousting anyone.

🟥 Mathematics of Greed: The Cong-tractor Economy of Edwin Gardiola  

1,193. 

That is the number of bank accounts the Court of Appeals ordered frozen in connection with one congressman and his contractor network, after the Anti-Money Laundering Council said those accounts may be tied to possible violations of the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act. 

Those 1,193 accounts sit inside a bigger picture: as of the latest order, total frozen assets in the flood control scandal have reached around P20.3 billion.  

These are bank accounts that accumulated from individual frozen financial lifelines, each one holding a piece of a money trail that had to be layered, moved, and hidden over years. 

A thousand accounts. Plus 193 more.  

I keep  repeating that because this is what the Lower House ignored and tolerated for years - because they protect each other. 

Let's start with this: Rep. Edwin Gardiola is the lawmaker linked to those accounts.  

🟥 The Contractor That Became Congress  

Gardiola wasn’t always in government. 

He made his money in construction, the kind that lives and dies on contracts from the Department of Public Works and Highways. 

From 2000 to 2008, during the Arroyo years, two family firms alone, JSG Construction and E. Gardiola Construction, reportedly secured around P11.6 billion in DPWH projects: 

- about P4.42 billion for JSG Construction, co-owned with his wife Judy

-  P7.21 billion for E. Gardiola Construction, owned by his brother Elmer and sister-in-law Elaine.  

Those contracts made his family one of the top DPWH contractors of that era. 

On paper, it looked like regular government contracting: private firms doing public works. In reality, it was already building the foundation of something much bigger.  

In 2012, the Bureau of Internal Revenue filed tax evasion charges against him. One detail from that case says everything. 

In 2006, Gardiola declared a gross taxable income of about P769,808 and paid only P1,856 in income tax. 

In the same year, he bought a 2003 BMW 745i11 for around P4.75 million. Between 2006 and 2009, records from the Land Transportation Office showed he bought several luxury vehicles, with total vehicle purchases reaching about P30.38 million, while he failed to even file income tax returns for 2007, 2008, and 2009.  

Imagine: while ordinary Filipinos quietly, religiously, consistently, and honestly pay their taxes, one rich, powerful construction magnate was allegedly cheating the system.

🟥 From Contractor to “Cong-tractor”  

Fast forward to 2022. After all those years of contracts and a live tax case on record, Gardiola enters the House of Representatives as the party-list representative of Construction Workers Solidarity.

The man whose family firms made billions off public works now becomes a lawmaker in a position to influence which projects get prioritized and funded.  

By then, the construction empire had changed form but not nature. 

After the old JSG and E. Gardiola Construction era, new companies connected to him and his relatives show up: Newington Builders Inc., Laurel Corp., and S-Ang Construction & General Trading. 

From 2016 to 2025, DPWH data show that companies linked to him secured around P39.78 billion in public works contracts:

- about P20.34 billion for Newington Builders - around P3.03 billion for Laurel
- roughly P16.40 billion for S-Ang. 

In total, they reportedly landed 358 contracts: 

- 188 for Newington
- 27 for Laurel 
- 143 for S-Ang 

That’s how the “cong-tractor” label follows him around. 

Not just a contractor. 
Not just a congressman.

A congressman whose orbit of companies keeps winning public infrastructure work year after year after year.

🟥 The Number That Changed Everything  

On July 22, 2025, in his State of the Nation Address, President Marcos ordered a crackdown on anomalous flood control projects. 

Months later, investigations pointed to ghost or non-existent projects, padded contracts, and a familiar set of names.  

By November 2025, an investigative body recommended graft and plunder charges against Gardiola and several other lawmakers. 

One key allegation from Batangas Rep. Leandro Leviste: that Gardiola had “pre-ordered” around P22 billion worth of projects, lining up flood control and infrastructure work even before proper congressional review of the budget could run its course.  

Then the Court of Appeals stepped in. 

First came earlier freeze orders in 2025. Then, in December 2025, a new order specifically targeted around 1,193 bank accounts tied to Gardiola and major contractors. 

Across all related orders, frozen assets in the flood control scandal totaled about P20.3 billion. 

The AMLC also flagged that, overall, the CA had issued freeze orders covering thousands more accounts and other assets in connection with the same probe.  

One man’s name is loud in the headlines. But it takes an entire ecosystem to generate 1,193 accounts and billions in contracts.  

🟥 The Pattern Nobody Stopped  

The part that's difficult to swallow is how long this pattern lived in public view. 

Back in 2009, the Philippine Center for Investigative Journalism was already reporting that JSG and E. Gardiola Construction were top DPWH contractors with billions in projects under Arroyo. 

In 2012, the BIR publicly charged him with attempting to evade taxes and failing to file returns while buying luxury vehicles like BMW, Lexus, Jaguar, and others.  

Between 2011 and 2017, while those issues hung in the background, Gardiola and his wife acquired three properties in Southern California. 

One was a Newport Beach mansion, estimated at close to P1 billion, a six-bedroom, nine-bathroom house on an 18,000-plus square foot lot overlooking the coast. 

The other two were homes in Ontario and Fontana, still under his name according to US property records.  

All of this existed: the DPWH contract history, the BIR complaints, the US properties. 

Yet in 2022, he wins a House seat.  

In 2022 alone, as he entered Congress, Newington Builders reportedly secured around P1.9 billion in DPWH contracts.  

That’s the pattern:  

- Billions in contracts from 2000 to 2008.  
- Luxury vehicles and undeclared income from 2006 to 2009.  
- Three US properties bought between 2011 and 2017.  
- Billions more in contracts from 2016 to 2025 through new firms.  
- A congressional seat in 2022.  
- A presidential order to investigate in 2025.  
- And only then, in late 2025, the 1,193 frozen bank accounts.  

🟥 The Real Crime  

Those 1,193 frozen accounts are the forensic aftermath. 

They show where money went, who moved it, which entities were used. But the deeper crime is how long the system lived comfortably with all the red flags.  

When a man can go from P769,808 in declared income and P1,856 in tax, to buying P30.38 million worth of luxury cars, to accumulating at least P11.6 billion in contracts under one administration, then another P39.78 billion under the next years, and still end up as a party-list representative for “construction workers,” something larger than one man is broken.  

The freeze order is the state finally saying “enough” on paper. But it came after decades of “go ahead” in practice.

So, think about it. 

You, the ordinary taxpayer, watching your salary get sliced by withholding tax every month, lining up for BIR deadlines, paying VAT on everything from sardines to school supplies, doing all of that quietly, religiously, consistently, and honestly. 

Meanwhile, a rich, powerful construction magnate with billions in government contracts and three houses in California somehow “forgets” to file income tax returns for years, buys luxury cars worth over P30 million, and ends up with 1,193 bank accounts frozen on suspicion of corruption. 

And for the longest time, the system still treated him better than it treats you.  

So question them. 

Every congressman, every senator, every DPWH official who talks about “projects” and “development” while floods keep rising and your taxes keep climbing. 

Do not accept their statements at face value, do not swallow their press releases as truth, and do not be ashamed of your anger. 

Be angry, be very angry, because these people are stealing from you in the open, and almost no one, least of all their friends in Congress or the Senate, is doing anything real to stop it.
