🟥 Philippine Politics in 2026: One Citizen’s Take

[An MCT Commentary]

I’m finally back home and because I didn’t want to sleep yet, attempting to restore my messed up body clock, I decided to review my notes for 2025 and write this piece.

We’re coming from a 2025 na puro sabog: 

- the UniTeam finally broke beyond repair, 

- Rodrigo Duterte was arrested on an ICC warrant and flown to The Hague, 

- Sara Duterte went through an impeachment process that the Supreme Court later voided, 

- the flood control scandal shook the system, and Marcos’ numbers slid hard in the surveys.  

Hindi ito expert analysis or consultant-level forecast. 

This is just one citizen’s take, from someone who’s been watching, writing, and worrying about where our politics is heading.

🟥 Marcos in 2026: Surviving, Not Thriving

By the end of 2025, Marcos walks into 2026 weaker than at any point in his presidency.

Survey numbers show his approval sliding into the low 30s, with almost half of respondents expressing disapproval or distrust, and particularly harsh ratings in Visayas and Mindanao. 

For a sitting president na hindi pa tapos ang term, mabigat yun.

A big driver of that anger is the flood control projects scandal.

Reports say around trillions have been poured into flood control in the last decade and a half, with a massive share suspected to have been lost to corruption. 

In his 2025 SONA, Marcos himself pointed out that about 100 billion pesos, roughly a fifth of his administration’s flood control spending, went to just 15 contractors, many linked to lawmakers and local officials.

Details that came out through media, leaks, and investigations include:

- Ghost projects and substandard flood defenses that failed as soon as typhoons hit  

- Insiders and contractors allegedly working together to inflate costs or fund non-existent works  

- Cash deliveries tied to powerful political figures, including then-Speaker Martin Romualdez and other legislators, mapped out in timelines and exposés

Government responded by forming a special commission, freezing some assets, and issuing lookout bulletins for certain contractors and personalities. 

Environmental and advocacy groups called it an obscene plunder of climate and disaster funds, since these projects were supposed to protect communities from floods.

On the streets, the Trillion Peso March turned frustration into bodies and banners. 

Massive rallies were held on dates heavy with symbolism, like September 21 and November 30, with people converging in Luneta, EDSA, and key provincial centers. 

Church groups, students, labor, professionals, and NGOs stood side by side, demanding punishment for the corrupt, recovery of stolen funds, stronger anti-corruption institutions, and serious political reform. 

Organizers were careful to keep it within constitutional lines, rejecting coup talk and calls for a military withdrawal of support.

Despite all this, by yearend only a few smaller fish and some poster contractors seemed genuinely at risk. The biggest names denied, lawyered up, or stayed very quiet.

Ang basa ko dito: Marcos tried to own the anti-corruption storyline by exposing the scandal, but he could not match the attempt with consequences. And Filipinos notice that gap.

On paper, 2026 is framed as part of economic recovery. In practice, the outlook is sobering. 

International lenders cut Philippine growth forecasts for 2025 and 2026, warning about slower expansion because of global factors, inflation, and domestic uncertainty. 

Projections land somewhere in the mid-single digits, which sounds fine for briefings, but does not guarantee better wages or lower prices.

Business groups keep calling 2026 a crucial year for reforms: cleaning up corruption, fixing infrastructure implementation, and tackling long-term problems like weak productivity and complicated rules. 

Congress ratified a record-high 2026 national budget, but critics point to questionable allocations, unprogrammed funds, and pork-like items hiding in technical language.

What this tells me: Marcos spends 2026 trying to prevent economic frustration from fully merging with political anger. 

The statistics may avoid full-blown crisis, but trust is already eroded. Without visible justice on corruption, even good policies will feel hollow.

🟥 The Duterte Camp: Bruised, Not Broken

While Marcos bleeds politically, the Duterte camp remains bruised, but very far from finished.

In March 2025, Rodrigo Duterte was arrested on an international warrant and transferred to The Hague, facing charges tied to the drug war. 

For human rights advocates, it felt like a long-delayed step toward accountability. 

For hardcore supporters, it was confirmation na ginigiba ang idol nila by foreign and local enemies.

Even in detention, his influence did not disappear. 

He won the mayoralty in Davao City in 2025, showing how deep his roots still are in his home base. 

The case abroad will move slowly, with motions, hearings, and political drama attached to every development.

Sara Duterte, meanwhile, had her own political crucifixion. 

The rift between the Marcos and Duterte camps turned from quiet tension into open feud. 

She broke away from the UniTeam, clashed with Martin Romualdez, and her camp was accused of plotting against the president’s inner circle. 

The House impeached her over confidential and intelligence funds, leading to a dramatic Senate trial that was eventually cut short when the Supreme Court voided the impeachment on procedural grounds.

Despite everything, her ratings stayed stronger than Marcos’. 

Nationally, she held relatively high approval and trust scores, and in Mindanao, her numbers were overwhelming while Marcos’ were dismal. 

Early 2028 preference polls consistently place her in the top slot, often with support in the low to mid 30s, while other figures trail far behind.

In the 2025 midterms, Duterte-aligned candidates performed better than many expected. 

Allies kept key positions in Mindanao and beyond. Pro-Duterte names in the Senate race remained loud and relevant. 

The message on the ground is clear: the Duterte brand is not just alive, it is organized.

Sa akin, parang ganito ang snapshot: even with an arrested patriarch and an almost-removed vice president, the Duterte machinery and narrative still have serious reach. 

The danger for them is not vanishing support, but accumulating baggage: corruption questions, foreign policy doubts, and the constant shadow of human rights cases.

🟥 Opposition And The Elusive Alternative

On paper, the opposition had some wins in 2025. In practice, it is still in the “sino ba talaga tayo” phase.

Analyses of the 2025 midterms highlight three things: 

- Marcos’ camp underperformed in the Senate,

- the Duterte bloc showed it could still swing votes, 

- reformist or opposition candidates scored meaningful gains in some local and party-list races. 

Voters sent a mixed message: pagod sa status quo, pero hindi pa buo ang tiwala sa alternatives.

Leni Robredo’s landslide win as mayor of Naga City became a symbol of localized hope. 

She launched a development roadmap for the city and quietly rebuilt from the ground up. 

Later on, her camp signaled she is unlikely to run for president in 2028, choosing to focus on Naga and her advocacies. 

That takes one big name out of the national equation, at least for now.

At the same time, there is growing talk of a progressive or democratic third force: a loose coalition of liberals, social democrats, youth groups, church networks, and civic organizations drawn together by issues like corruption, human rights, education, and dynasties. 

The Trillion Peso March became a key test space for this: if they can coordinate on the streets, can they coordinate at the ballot box?

Names floated for 2028 from this side include Risa Hontiveros, Bam Aquino, and some younger local executives and advocates whose public profiles rose in 2025. 

But everything is couched in maybe, if unity happens, if surveys improve. Walang solid anchor pa.

The pattern I see: 2026 is their organizing exam. 

If they stay as scattered brands and small parties, they will be drowned out by Duterte machinery and whatever is left of the Marcos coalition. 

If they manage to agree on a shared banner and minimum agenda, they become a real alternative for voters who are sick of dynastic ping-pong.

🟥 Trillion Peso March And The 2026 Mood

The Trillion Peso March is not just a one-off protest. It is a mood that might carry into 2026.

It tied together multiple frustrations:

- Corruption in flood control and other infrastructure  

- Climate disasters made worse by missing or substandard projects  

- Dynasties and their grip on budgets  

- A feeling that government is captured by syndicates, not public servants

You had priests and pastors on stage, students on the streets, middle-class professionals carrying witty but angry placards, and workers marching shoulder to shoulder. 

The organizing language was anti-corruption, anti-dynasty, pro-accountability. 

Calls for military intervention were explicitly rejected, which is important in a country with a history of coups and people power.

The way it lands on me: people are trying to push the system to clean itself up without breaking it. 

That is a very Filipino compromise instinct. It is also very fragile. 

If 2026 brings more scandals with no consequences, expect bigger, angrier, and more frequent mobilizations.

🟥 2026 As The Quiet Start Of 2028

The way I see it, 2026 is already part of the 2028 presidential race.

Early preference surveys and political columns line up Sara Duterte as the frontrunner, with other names scattered far behind. 

Some potential candidates will fade, others will suddenly appear, but the basic framing is there: Duterte versus whoever dares to challenge her, with Marcos trying not to look irrelevant, and the opposition trying not to implode.

In that context:

- Marcos will spend 2026 trying to avoid becoming a lame duck, even as his scandals and low ratings weigh down his endorsements.  

- Sara will keep sharpening the story that everyone is out to destroy her because she is strong, using impeachment, her father’s case, and attacks from Manila elites as proof.  

- The opposition will either do the slow, tiring work of unifying, or remain background noise in another season of dynastic drama.  

- Corruption, the ICC, and economic frustration will quietly color how people judge all of them, even when campaign language is full of unity, discipline, or strong leadership.

For people like us, ordinary citizens and  pro good governance writers, 2026 will not give a neat resolution.  

Pero it will reveal character.  

Who protects their own at all costs.  

Who throws allies under the bus to save themselves.  

Who actually listens when people march, and who hides behind statements and spokespeople.

That is where this take really lives this year: not in grand predictions, but in watching who chooses what, when it finally matters.

₱1.5 million break bonus daw.

What does that even mean? And why are we all acting like this is fine?

1. Let’s start with the ₱1.5M

Batangas Rep. Leandro Leviste says that on the day the Lower House was supposed to vote on the 2026 budget in October 2025, 1.5 million pesos was being given out to lawmakers.  

Same day as a crucial budget vote, same place, and money is moving quietly through the building for congressmen.

He also says lawmakers do not just get regular monthly funds. There are extra amounts in October and December, conveniently timed with budget approval moments.  

So that 1.5 million pesos people are talking about is one of those “break” payouts during the Undas or budget period, separate from the later December “Christmas bonus” talk.

And the more you sit with that, the dirtier it feels.

2. Then Toby Tiangco drops the real bomb

Navotas Rep. Toby Tiangco comes in and basically says: yes, this happens, and it has been happening for years.

He admits that every time the Lower House goes on break, lawmakers receive what he calls a break bonus or allowance.  

He even says this has been going on since at least 2010, when he first became a congressman. This is not a new thing under the current administration. This is standard Lower House behavior.

He explains the pattern like this:

- Lawmakers get monthly MOOE, the usual office funds.  

- On top of that, they get extra money when the House goes on recess: Undas, Christmas, Holy Week, and other breaks.  

- He openly confirms he received 1.5 million pesos for the Undas break.

And the process? They sign a voucher, they get the checks, and these particular break amounts do not require detailed liquidation.

That is not an accident. That is a system designed to move money without real scrutiny.

3. Then there’s the 2 million “not-a-bonus” bonus

Leviste also talks about another amount: around 2 million pesos per lawmaker in December, after the bicam report on the 2026 budget was ratified. He calls it a Christmas bonus for some House members.

The response from House leaders is almost insulting:

- They say this is not a Christmas bonus, but MOOE, office money, standard allocation.  

- They insist the funds are for district activities, Christmas events, consultations, and programs.  

- Others claim the checks seen in photos are meant for staff salaries and operations, not a bonus for lawmakers.

So they play this word game:

- 1.5 million in October? Break allowance.  

- 2 million in December? Office funds only, definitely not a bonus.  

- Yet both are sent in checks, both are handled inside the same opaque internal budget, and both sit on top of an already bloated MOOE.

They keep insisting it is legal. Maybe it is. That is the point. They turned legal structures into tools to quietly reward themselves.

4. This is what “legal corruption” looks like

Look at the pattern and tell me this is not theft with paperwork.

- The Lower House’s internal budget jumps by 10.5 billion pesos, up to 27.7 billion, with no clear public explanation.  

- MOOE alone hits 18.58 billion, with a huge chunk listed as “other maintenance and operating expenses,” vague and loaded, often liquidated by certification instead of real receipts.  

- Extra money drops in October and December, in the middle of and right after major budget decisions.  

- A staffer even shows Leviste a 151 million peso package and calls it his incentive before a vote.

They are not just breaking rules. They are writing the rules in a way that lets them grab more while still being able to say, “It’s allowed. It’s audited. It’s office funds.”

That is why it stings: they can say it is legal while everyone can see it is abusive.  
It is legal corruption, dressed up as MOOE, liquidations by certification, and break allowances.

And the worst part? They know most people will shrug and move on.

5. The part that really makes me angry

Tiangco saying this has been happening since 2010 is supposed to calm us down, like: relax, this is normal, this is how the Lower House works.  
For me, that makes it worse.

Because if this has been the habit for more than a decade, that means:

- We have been funding these break bonuses for years without being told they exist.  

- They are so used to it that they talk about 1.5 million and 2 million like it is just another day at work.  

- We are now at a point where they can openly admit it on TV and still expect nothing to change.

They keep finding new labels, new excuses, new categories in the budget. Office funds. Programs. For the district.  
We keep paying for it through taxes, higher prices, and broken services.

And most of us are so numb we are not even angry anymore. We just swallow it.  

We share a link. 
We rant for one night. 
Then we move on.

Meanwhile, they stay. They sign another voucher. Another check. Another break. Another not-a-bonus.

All legal.

And now, after staring at this mess in the Lower House, there is one more question I cannot shake:

If this is what is happening here,  
what does it look like on the Senate side?

The other question: how come I am hearing about this from Tiangco and Leviste, congressmen I gave zero trust to?

How come I am not hearing about it, loudly and clearly, from the progressive reformists in the Lower House — the Makabayan bloc, Gabriela, ACT Teachers, Kabataan, people like France Castro, Arlene Brosas, and Raoul Manuel?

This comment was left earlier under my blog about Leandro Leviste. 

The wording alone made my ears ring. 

I want to give this comment the focus it deserves because when you multiply this kind of mindset by millions, you start to see why the Philippines is in real deep shit.  

"At least he spoke and opened about corruption."  

On the surface, it sounds harmless. Supportive pa nga. 

But this is exactly the mindset that keeps corruption alive in the Philippines: contento na sa bare minimum, then shocked when politicians treat us like basura. 

This is the same spirit as pwede na yan. Pwede na ang speech. Pwede na ang pa-strong words. Pwede na ang “may guts.” Even if walang track record, walang policy, walang malinaw na plano.  

🟥 How This Mindset Feeds Corruption  

Corruption in the Philippines survives not only because of powerful people, but because ordinary citizens keep excusing them.  

When we say “at least he spoke,” we reward words, not work.  

When we say “bata pa siya, may guts,” we treat age and attitude as qualification, instead of competence and integrity.  

When we make excuses for politicians we like, we send the message that they can abuse power and still keep our support.  

Look at what happened under Rodrigo Duterte. 

Despite killings, controversies, and scandals, he stayed popular because many framed him as “at least may political will,” “at least anti-drugs,” as if toughness cancels out abuse and impunity.  

Now look at Sara Duterte. 

She faces serious questions about how public funds were used in her offices, yet some still say, “at least matapang,” “at least nagpalabas ng statement,” as if PR and legal defense equal innocence.  

Same formula. Different Duterte.

🟥 Low Standards, Low-Quality Politicians  

When you tolerate low standards, you attract low-quality leaders.  

Cavite Rep. Kiko Barzaga is a good example. 

Instead of modeling maturity, he became known for reckless social media behavior and had to face House sanctions for it. 

This is an elected official, handling laws, budgets, and public trust, yet acting like a clout-chasing influencer.  

If your reaction is “at least he speaks against Marcos” or “at least he fights,” you are proving to politicians that they can be irresponsible, sexist, or dishonest, and still keep fans as long as they push the right emotional buttons.  

They learn fast.  

We teach them what they can get away with.  

🟥 Raising The Bar, Not Just Raising Our Voice  

Here’s the standard, at least on my page:  

Speaking about corruption is the starting line, not the finish line.  

You don’t get extra credit for doing what is basic moral duty.  

“Guts” without competence, ethics, and consistency is just drama.  

If a young politician speaks against corruption, good. 

Question them pa rin:  

Ano na ang batas na na-file?  
Ano ang risk na tinanggap niya?  
Sino ang hinabla?  
Ano ang nabago sa sistema?  

If the answer is “wala pa, but may potential,” then treat him as what he is: a work in progress, not a hero.  

In fact, never treat any politician as a hero. Ever. 

🟥 This Page Is a Coaching Zone

So yes, when you drop comments like “at least he spoke” on my posts, I will treat them as raw material for a bigger conversation.  

When you comment something like that on any of my posts, expect that when I see it, I will turn it into a coaching opportunity, a lesson to learn.  

Not to embarrass you.  
Not to flex being “woke.”  

But because this is how we unlearn pwede na yan thinking. 

Because every time we let that mindset pass, we help create the next Duterte, the next Barzaga, the next trapo who thinks Filipinos will always settle for scraps.  

If you stay on this page, expect this:  

Your comments are welcome. Your opinions are welcome.  

But your standards will be challenged.

Rizal was right about the youth being the hope of the nation. But he never said all youth, all the time, no matter what they do.  

🟥 When “pag-asa ng bayan” looks like clout  

There are days when that Rizal quote feels like an emotional scam.  

You grow up hearing “ang kabataan ang pag-asa ng bayan,” then you open your feed and see a congressman meowing in sessions, livestreaming his feelings, and getting suspended by the House ethics committee for reckless posts that supposedly harm the integrity of the institution.  

Yes, that is also “kabataan.”  

You see a young lawmaker turning his quirky “meow” persona into a brand of Gen Z politics, using memes and livestreams to rail against corruption and even call for the president’s resignation. 

On paper, that sounds brave. In practice, the line between courage and spectacle gets very thin when your politics depends on virality.  

So you sit there thinking: if this is the sample size of kabataan, tangina, talo ang Pilipinas.

🟥 The Leviste kind of “pag-asa”  

Then there is the other type.  

Leandro Leviste, batang congressman, business background, talks about reducing political positions through an “anti-politician bill” and brands himself as someone willing to shrink the political class. 

He made headlines exposing alleged bribery in flood control projects, filing cases after an entrapment operation over millions in supposed bribe money.  

Sounds promising, no?  

But you read deeper and you see the patterns that bother you. 

His outrage can look selective: loud in one corruption narrative, quiet in another where money is allegedly tied to an ally’s projects. 

Now there is news that he could face an ethics complaint himself over how he handled sensitive DPWH files tied to a former undersecretary who later died, with sources claiming those documents were taken forcibly.  

So on one side, may meow politics. On the other, curated outrage politics.  

Pareho silang bata. Pareho silang malakas ang dating online. Pareho ring questionable ang credibility at  ethics.  🤷‍♂️

This is where the despair creeps in.  

🟥 Rizal’s youth versus our influencers  

Rizal wrote “A la Juventud Filipina” to urge youth to use their talents in arts, science, and civic life to lift the country from oppression. 

The hope was not about age alone, it was about a certain quality of youth: educated, principled, willing to think of the country beyond themselves.  

The problem now is this:  

- The system rewards visibility more than virtue. Young politicians gain power by mastering social media, not by proving long-term service.  

- Parties and dynasties still decide who gets a shot. Being “pag-asa ng bayan” becomes more about surname, network, and PR than about track record and integrity.  

- Voters are tired and angry, so anything that looks like “galit sa corruption” feels enough, even if the details are messy, incomplete, or conveniently targeted.  

So we get youthful faces acting out very old patterns. New packaging, same traditional politics.  

And yet, Rizal’s line still echoes, because outside Congress, there are students, workers, campus journalists, and community organizers who take that quote seriously. 

Youth who study history, fight disinformation, join rallies, and insist that hindi kami lang ang future, kami na ang ngayon na bayan.  

We just do not always put them on TV.  

🟥 What kind of youth we should actually be voting for  

If we expect more from the youth, then we have to be more specific about what “pag-asa” looks like in 2025 and beyond.  

For public life, youth worth betting on should look less like influencers and more like boring, steady workers with receipts. As in:  

- Consistent values, not just consistent engagement  
  
Someone who will call out corruption whether the suspect is enemy or ally, with the same intensity, and with documents, not just tears and mic drops.  

- Respect for institutions, even while criticizing them  
  
Yes, call for resignations if needed. But do not drag entire institutions into chaos for clout, or encourage the military to act in ways that blur democratic lines.  

- Transparent handling of power and information  
  
Sensitive files, leaks, and “whistleblowing” should follow ethical processes, not become props in a public performance that may put people or cases at risk.  

- Willingness to be boring  
  
Real reform work is committee hearings, long drafting sessions, consultations, coalition-building. The kind of work that never trends but changes lives.  

And honestly, youth leaders who know when to say “hindi ko alam, kailangan ko pang aralin yan” are far more hopeful than those who always have a viral line ready.  

🟥 Our responsibility as the ones watching  

The country is not hopeless. It is just badly curated.  

Rizal did not say “ang kahit sinong batang may ring light ang pag-asa ng bayan.” The line has always assumed a filter: education, ethics, service, courage, and a willingness to put the country above ego.  

So hindi automatically “pag-asa ng bayan” ang kabataan. 

May qualifier si Rizal: the good and honest youth, the ones who actually give their time, talent, and future to the country, not to their brand.  

Kung may batang meow, batang anti-corruption kuno, batang dynasty baby, fine. They exist.  

Pero hindi sila template.  

Tayo pa rin ang magdedesisyon, every election, every share, every like, kung sino sa kabataan ang tatawagin nating pag-asa, at sino ang isa lang sa many, many warnings ng history para hindi tayo madaling mauto. 

Tonight, malinaw  sa akin kung sino ang hindi.

I'm on a WestJet flight, en route to Los Angeles from Calgary, and I was just thinking about 2026 and how to move forward as MCT. 

Not in a grand way, but in a quiet way: sitting with my own doubts, wondering if this space is still useful, and asking what it should actually be doing in a country drowning in online noise.  

Because the more I look around, the clearer it feels that this can’t stay as “rant lang.” 

Not when lies are getting harder to spot and more people are openly worried about fake and misleading content online. 

If MCT continues in 2026, it can’t just be louder. It has to be clearer, calmer, and more intentional.  

🟥 What this space has quietly been doing  

If you’ve been here the past year, you know this space hasn’t just been giving takes. It’s been quietly teaching.  

All this time, MCT has been showing how to ask better questions instead of swallowing the first version of the story. 

It has been nudging you to be picky with what you read and share. 

It has been using links, reports, and screenshots to help you connect dots, not just collect “tea.” 

It has been pushing you to keep asking deeper questions, even when the easy answer already feels satisfying.  

That is critical thinking, but in everyday form. 

It sits in how you scroll, what you click, what you ignore, and what you decide to pass on to others. 

It matters, because most people are meeting disinformation in the same place they get their news and relax: social media. 

So even a “small” blog like this becomes part of that same information world, whether it wants to or not.  

🟥 What we’ve learned since 2016  

Since 2016, we have seen the same pattern again and again. 

Elections arrive. 

Lies and half-truths spread faster. 

Trolls, influencers, and “organic” fans share the same lines. 

People fight in the comments and in group chats. 

After that, everyone is tired and confused.  

We have also seen what does not work. 

Bardagulan made us feel brave for a while, but it mostly fed the algorithm and gave troll networks more content to use. 

The elitist tone, the “ang bobo niyo” mood, did not teach anyone; it just made the gap wider and confirmed the picture of “sila yung nagmamagaling.” 

Dismissive reactions, the “obvious namang fake ’yan” replies, were careless, because clearly, for many people, it was not obvious at all. 

Silence out of fear felt safe, but in a system built on lies, silence starts to look like agreement.  

During all this, the disinformation machine became more organized. 

Many campaigns are planned like ad campaigns, with teams, budgets, and timelines. 

They do not just show up during election month. They plan years ahead.  

So as we enter 2026, with 2028 already on the horizon, it is not honest to say “things will sort themselves out.”  

🟥 The plan for 2026  

For 2026, MCT does not need to be bigger. It needs to be more clear about its work.  

The first part of the plan is to teach questions, not just opinions. Each post should leave you with simple, useful questions you can bring anywhere: how do I know this, who gains if I believe this, and what might be missing here.  

The second part is to make checking sources feel normal. When we talk about an issue, you will see links and context. Not to drown you in details, but to show that checking is something any person can do, not only experts.  

The third part is to connect patterns, not just react to events. Instead of chasing every viral scandal, this space will keep pointing out how the same tricks, stories, and networks keep coming back from 2016 to now.  

The fourth part is to drop the elitist energy for good. There is no place here for talking down to people or hinting that only a few are “smart enough.” The aim is to help more people understand, not to impress a small, already convinced group.  

The fifth part is to name what does not help. Bardagulan, mockery, and silence will not be treated as “strategy” anymore. They will be seen for what they are: ways we cope when we are angry or scared, but not ways that fix the problem.  

The sixth part is to prepare quietly for 2028. Every post in 2026 is part of a longer effort: making you harder to fool when the next wave of trolls, fake stories, and staged “movements” start warming up for the next national fight.  

🟥 Accepting the limits of this space  

There is another honest part I cannot skip: MCT is a crowdfunded advocacy. 

It runs on time, energy, and work that real people choose to support. 

If one day the support slows down or stops, MCT may have to go quiet. 

If that happens, it will not be a scandal. 

It will simply mean this small corner has done what it could, for as long as it could.  

And that is okay. 

This page was never meant to carry the whole fight. 

Long before MCT existed, there were journalists, fact-checkers, teachers, organizers, researchers, and creators already pushing back against lies and helping people think. 

They will still be here even if this space has to fade. We are not the center of the advocacy. 

We are part of a much bigger line of people doing the same work in different ways.  

Knowing this makes the work in 2026 feel lighter and more focused. The goal is not to build something that must live forever. The goal is to use whatever time and support we still have to help you build habits and instincts that will stay with you, even if one day this page is gone.  

🟥 What readers can expect from me  

From your side of the screen, this means a few things.  

You can expect posts that explain how to look at a story, not just what to feel about it. 

You can expect pieces that are slower and more reflective, not only reactions to whatever is trending in the last 24 hours. 

You can expect honest moments of “I don’t know yet” when the information is not complete. 

You can expect that when a take turns out to be wrong or unfair, it will be corrected, not quietly deleted and forgotten.  

The online world will keep changing. Lies will keep trying to look cleaner and more normal. 

But more people are also trying to double-check, to learn tools, to be more careful before they share. 

This blog wants to stand on that side: the side that is tired of being played and is ready to think a little deeper.  

🟥 What I hope from you  

This cannot be a one-way thing. MCT cannot do this alone.  

In 2026, it would help a lot if readers send a message when something feels unclear, not to attack, but to ask for a source or an update. 

It helps when you share posts not only because they match your feelings, but because they also pass your own small checks.

It matters when you bring what you learn here into your own spaces, whether that is a family thread, an office chat, or a small group in your community.  

The other side has money, tools, and organized plans. What we have are habits, questions, and each other.  

So the plan for 2026 is simple. MCT will keep writing in a way that helps you think, not just react. 

It will keep connecting dots across years and elections. It will keep saying no to shortcuts that feel good for a moment but leave us just as lost.  

If there is one line to bring into the new year, maybe it is this:  

Don’t just consume information. Learn how to fight with it, think with it, and see through it.
